requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-09-28T16:49:14Z","updated_at":"2024-11-08T02:19:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55920","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"We have `!f === (!)∘f` to negate any boolean function. Was there ever discussion of making `-f === (-)∘f` too? Both are small conveniences to avoid defining `x -> op(x)`. This one would allow for example:

```julia
julia> A = reshape(1:16,4,4);

julia> eigvals(A; sortby=-real)  # does not accept rev=true
4-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
     36.209372712298546 + 0.0im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 + 1.9341169324764503e-16im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 - 1.9341169324764503e-16im
     -2.209372712298546 + 0.0im

julia> sum(-log, A; dims=1)  # -sum allocates twice
1×4 Matrix{Float64}:
 -3.17805  -7.42655  -9.38261  -10.6846
```

This will not change the fact that `-f.(xs)` means `-(f.(xs))`, when usually fused `@. -f(xs)` would be better. (Unless you write `(-f).(xs)` by hand.)

Needs tests obviously, but RFC?

The meaning of a generic function should not change depending on the type of the value that is passed to the function. The expression `!x` normally means "negate the logical value `x`". But for `x::Function` it means `(!) ∘ x`.

Likewise, we should not have two separate meanings for unary `-`:

```julia
-x == -1 * x

-x == (-) ∘ x   # If `x` is a `Function`. 
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2463624998/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

public","user":{"login":"aplavin","id":687995,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4Nzk5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/687995?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin","html_url":"https://github.com/aplavin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aplavin/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":421481488,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw0MjE0ODE0ODg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/needs%20news","name":"needs here!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55129/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55129/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2474095511","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55129#issuecomment-2474095511","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55129","id":2474095511,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Td7OX","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-13T16:21:05Z","updated_at":"2024-11-13T16:21:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"My comparison to the `setindex!` docstring was only meant to demonstrate what a generic docstring looks like, but I guess it was not the best example. The `iterate` docstring is a better example:

```julia
    iterate(iter [, state]) -> Union{Nothing, Tuple{Any, Any}}

Advance the iterator to obtain the next element. If no elements remain, `nothing` should
be returned. Otherwise, a 2-tuple of the next element and the new iteration state
should be returned.
```

Note that the docstring provides a single definition for `iterate` regardless of the input types.

But you bring up an interesting point. In my previous comment I explicitly ignored the second docstring for `setindex!`. So, `setindex!` actually has at least three distinct behaviors, depending on the input type:

*Throw a bounds error for an array:*

```julia
julia> x = [1, 2];

julia> x[3] = 10
ERROR: BoundsError: attempt to access 2-element Vector{Int64} at index [3]
```

*Extend a dictionary:*

```julia
julia> d = Dict(:a => 1);

julia> d[:b] = 2
2
```

*Set multiple indices at once for an array:*

```julia
julia> a = [1, 2, 3, 4];

julia> a[[2, 3]] = [10, 20];

julia> a
4-element Vector{Int64}:
  1
 10
 20
  4
```

So, `setindex!` is a very non-generic function. I guess that's ok, since `setindex!` is a bit special due to the square-bracket syntax sugar and it's importance for array indexing. Not every function in Julia has to be a generic function. There are some notable exceptions to the goal of having generic functions, like constructors and `convert`, where the exact behavior depends on the input type. But I think we should generally prefer to make Base Julia functions generic whenever possible. `Base.setindex` does not have syntax sugar and it is not ubiquitously used like `setindex!`, so I think it makes sense to make it generic (and we can add `setkey` if desired).

> So the consequence would be to write a docstring for `setindex` that does not talk about index bounds at all for consistency's sake?

No, that's not the consequence. Please take a look at the example docstrings that I provided in my previous post.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2474095511/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

ambiguous","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":12995770,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMjk5NTc3MA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/labels/Documentation","name":"Documentation","color":"0b02e1","default":false,"description":null},{"id":3631152487,"node_id":"LA_kwDOAAzd1s7Ybv1n","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/labels/Needs%20Triage","name":"Needs Triage","color":"5E33EA","default":false,"description":"Issue requires triage"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-23T15:13:25Z","updated_at":"2024-09-23T15:13:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue linked to the documentation

The documentation for the `**params` parameter to the `fit` method of `GridSearchCV` leads to confusion. Here is the current text:


> Parameters passed to the `fit` method of the estimator, the scorer, and the CV splitter.
> 
> If a fit parameter is an array-like whose length is equal to `num_samples` then it will be split across CV groups along with `X` and `y`. For example, the [sample_weight](https://scikit-learn.org/dev/glossary.html#term-sample_weight) parameter is split because `len(sample_weights) = len(X)`.

I was worried that this meant that `grid_search.fit(X, y, groups=g)` would split `g` up across the CV partitions, which is definitely not the right behavior. The correct behavior is to pass the `groups` parameter unchanged to the CV splitter, e.g. `cv.split(X, y, groups=groups)`. I read through the source code and it does appear that the `groups` parameter will get passed through unchanged to `split`, so it looks like the behavior is correct. But we could use something in the docstring that clarifies this behavior.

### Suggest a potential alternative/fix

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350","id":2445397185,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6RwczB","number":55350,"title":"Explicit calls to type inference in generators or array comprehensions cause equivalent expressions to have different values","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-02T16:48:34Z","updated_at":"2024-08-02T16:48:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm sure this issue is very well known among core developers, but I though it wouldn't hurt to open a Github issue to track it. If this is already covered by a different open issue, feel free to close this one.

Julia is a dynamic language, so the value of an expression should only depend on the runtime values of the components of the expression. However, this is not always the case when using generators or array comprehensions, which I think is because the implementation uses `Core.Compiler.return_type` (or similar) somewhere along the line when the input collections are empty. (Sorry, I haven't actually read through the relevant code lately, but I've looked at it in the past.)

Here's a simple example of two expressions where the components of the expression have the exact same values, but the values of the expressions are different:

Define input values:

```julia
const a = Int[]
const b = Int[]
c = Int[]
d = Int[]
g(::Vector{Int}) = 1
g(::Vector{Any}) = 2
```

Evaluate expressions:

```julia
julia> a == c, b == d
(true, true)

julia> g([x*y for x in a for y in b])
1

julia> g([x*y for x in c for y in d])
2
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55350/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

described [on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/juliaup-modifications-to-zshrc/118844), I made the mistake of overwriting my `.zshrc` file (on MacOS), after which I could no longer run the If it was supposed to be private in Base, why is it exported to Base?

`@with` is used in a couple places in Base, and also one place in the unit tests. (I mean in addition to the unit tests for the ScopedValues module.)

`with` and `@with` are extremely generic names, which is why I think some folks would like to keep those names contained in `Base.ScopedValues`. My preferred access pattern is either of the following:

```julia
using Base.ScopedValues

#or:

using Base.ScopedValues: ScopedValue, with
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2226254429/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm not a fan of this, because that means a symbol could be public on 1.10 (because it's in the manual), private on 1.11 (because it's not declared with `public`), and then public again on 1.12 (because it gets declared with `public` later on). We should be encouraging less usage of private symbols, not more. Anything on 1.11 that is not declared with `export` or `public` is [*officially* private](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.11.0-beta1/doc/src/manual/faq.md#public-apiid-man-api).

In fact, making a public symbol private in a minor release is a breaking change according to SemVer, so this issue should block release of 1.11.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2125139239/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Are there exceptions where you are accessing non-public API? I at least think (then) we need a list of modules that you can and should do such for. [Or it this taken care of by "public" rather than "export", for all methods in such modules? Is a module ok to use in full or in part?]

It might not be super common in practice, but it's perfectly valid to do `using Base.Iterators` (or `using .Iterators`). It's also perfectly valid to do `using Base.ScopedValues`. Submodules need to have their own `export` and `public` declarations for the symbols within the submodules.

However, I think we probably do need to add `public` declarations in `Base` for both the `Iterators` submodule and the `ScopedValues` submodule, which I commented on [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51335#issuecomment-2125147434).

> Is this something we wanna have or should this get closed?

In my opinion, this PR should be closed. The recommend approach is to use qualified names, like `ScopedValues.with`, or to bring the names into scope with `using`, e.g.

```julia
using Base.ScopedValues
```

or

```julia
using Base.ScopedValues: with
```

However, you could modify the PR title and make the change in this PR the addition of `Iterators` and `ScopedValues` to the `public` list.

🙂

I think `Base.ScopedValues` is fine.

Do you think `Base.ScopedValues.ScopedValue(x)` is fine? Or `Base.ScopedValues.with`? I think exporting `ScopedValues` makes sense given that we do the same thing for commonly used modules like `Iterators`, `Threads`, and `Sys`. "Why clutter the namespace?" is an argument that I'm getting tired of hearing. It lacks substance. Why not make ScopedValues convenient and ergonomic to use?

But yeah, the situation with ScopedValues.jl is unfortunate.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2125917092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

And then your sparse vector is corrupted:

```julia
julia> using SparseArrays

julia> x = SparseVector(5, collect(1:2:7), collect(1:4))
5-element SparseVector{Int64, Int64} with 4 stored entries:
  [1]  =  1
  [3]  =  2
  [5]  =  3
  [7]  =  4

julia> filter!(iseven, x)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching deleteat!(::SparseVector{Int64, Int64}, ::UnitRange{Int64})

Closest candidates are:
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::AbstractUnitRange{<:Integer})
   @ Base array.jl:1525
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::AbstractVector)
   @ Base array.jl:1567
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::Any)
   @ Base array.jl:1566
  ...

Stacktrace:
 [1] filter!(f::typeof(iseven), a::SparseVector{Int64, Int64})
   @ Base .\array.jl:2662
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1

julia> x
5-element SparseVector{Int64, Int64} with 6 stored entries:
  [1]  =  0
  [2]  =  2
  [3]  =  0
  [4]  =  3
  [5]  =  3
  [7]  =  4
```

This issue can happen whenever you have a generic function that temporarily causes its arguments to be in an invalid state - because it's always a possibility that the later operations that restore the correct state to the argument throws an error.

To more specifically respond to your question, I would expect the following to work, but it does not:

```julia
julia> x = sparsevec(1:2:7, 1:4, 8)
8-element SparseVector{Int64, Int64} with 4 stored entries:
  [1]  =  1
  [3]  =  2
  [5]  =  3
  [7]  =  4

julia> x[3]
2

julia> try
           filter!(iseven, x)
       catch
       end

julia> x[3]
0
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2037166769/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

-Since `with` requires a closure or a function and creates another call-frame,
-it can sometimes be beneficial to use the macro form.
+`ScopedValues` provides a macro version of `with`. The expression `@with var=>val expr`
+evaluates `expr` in a new dynamic scope with `var` set to `val`. `@with var=>val expr`
+is equivalent to `with(var=>val) do expr end`. However, `with` requires a zero-argument
+closure or function, which results in an extra call-frame. If you wish to avoid the
+extra call-frame, then you can use the macro form. #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion #53471. This should probably be backported to 1.11.

One thing to note, I changed the signature in the `with` docstring from this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val::T)...)
```

to this:

```julia
with(f, (var::ScopedValue{T} => val)...)
```

...since the original signature in the docstring was too strict. I also added this sentence to the docstring:

```julia
`val` will be converted to type `T`.
```

I added a couple tests that verify the conversion {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644","id":2174448303,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Bm3Kv","number":53644,"title":"Consider renaming `ScopedValue` to `ContextValue`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-03-07T18:04:55Z","updated_at":"2024-03-07T18:04:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Introduction

The new `ScopedValue` feature is really nice, but I think the name and the description of the feature in terms of dynamic scope is likely to lead to confusion.

There was a [claim on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/naming-of-scopedvalues/110747/2?u=cameronbieganek) that the PR had "an extensive analysis and bikeshedding of the naming". However, I have read through the entire PR and I disagree with that statement. There is some discussion of the name, but it is not a very complete discussion. In particular, it is surprising that there was no discussion regarding using the terminology "context" instead of "dynamic scope". In PR #35833, @tkf pointed out that the "context" terminology is used in at least four major programming languages:

- Python: [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html)
- .NET: [`ExecutionContext`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1)
- Kotlin: [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html)
- Go: [`Context`](https://go.dev/blog/context)

Julia is a lexically scoped language, and the Scoped Values PR does not change that. Below are a couple examples that I think emphasize that what `ScopedValue` provides is *not* dynamic scoping.

(Disclaimer: I have only tested these using ScopedValues.jl. I need to install `juliaup` so I can get nightly.)

## Example 1

```julia
julia> using ScopedValues

julia> f() = a[];

julia> function g()
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           f()
       end;

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main ./REPL[2]:1
 [2] g()
   @ Main ./REPL[3]:3
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Example 2

```julia
julia> module A
           f() = a[]
           export f
       end
Main.A

julia> module B
           using ScopedValues
           using ..A
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           g() = f()
           export g
       end
Main.B

julia> using .B

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main.A ./REPL[1]:2
 [2] g()
   @ Main.B ./REPL[2]:5
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Discussion

Both of these examples would work if `ScopedValue` provided true dynamic scoping. So essentially we are telling the user that Julia uses a subtle combination of lexical scoping and dynamic scoping. I think that's more confusing than it needs to be. We should describe this feature as a "context" feature rather than a "dynamic scoping" feature. This is about more than just the name, it is about our semantic understanding of what the feature provides.

So, after a renaming, this feature could look like this:

```julia
f() = a[]
a = ContextValue(1)

newcontext(a => 2) do
    f()
end # returns 2
```

Of course the precise naming is up for debate. Possible names for the context entering function:

- `with`
- `withcontext`
- `context`
- `incontext`
- `updatecontext`
- `newcontext`

I think `updatecontext` and `newcontext` are probably the most accurate names to describe what they do.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

The new `ScopedValue` feature is really nice, but I think the name and the description of the feature in terms of dynamic scope is likely to lead to confusion.

There was a [claim on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/naming-of-scopedvalues/110747/2?u=cameronbieganek) that the PR had "an extensive analysis and bikeshedding of the naming". However, I have read through the entire PR and I disagree with that statement. There is some discussion of the name, but it is not a very complete discussion. In particular, it is surprising that there was no discussion regarding using the terminology "context" instead of "dynamic scope". In PR #35833, @tkf pointed out that the "context" terminology is used in at least four major programming languages:

- Python: [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html)
- .NET: [`ExecutionContext`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1)
- Kotlin: [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html)
- Go: [`Context`](https://go.dev/blog/context)

Julia is a lexically scoped language, and the Scoped Values PR does not change that. Below are a couple examples that I think emphasize that what `ScopedValue` provides is *not* dynamic scoping.

(Disclaimer: I have only tested these using ScopedValues.jl. I need to install `juliaup` so I can get nightly.)

## Example 1

```julia
julia> using ScopedValues

julia> f() = a[];

julia> function g()
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           f()
       end;

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main ./REPL[2]:1
 [2] g()
   @ Main ./REPL[3]:3
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Example 2

```julia
julia> module A
           f() = a[]
           export f
       end
Main.A

julia> module B
           using ScopedValues
           using ..A
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           g() = f()
           export g
       end
Main.B

julia> using .B

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main.A ./REPL[1]:2
 [2] g()
   @ Main.B ./REPL[2]:5
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Discussion

Both of these examples would work if `ScopedValue` provided true dynamic scoping. So essentially we are telling the user that Julia uses a subtle combination of lexical scoping and dynamic scoping. I think that's more confusing than it needs to be. We should describe this feature as a "context" feature rather than a "dynamic scoping" feature. This is about more than just the name, it is about our semantic understanding of what the feature provides.

So, after a renaming, this feature could look like this:

```julia
f() = a[]
a = ContextValue(1)

newcontext(a => 2) do
    f()
end # returns 2
```

Of course the precise naming is up for debate. Possible names for the context entering function:

- `with`
- `withcontext`
- `context`
- `incontext`

That's a bit rude and discourages feedback and contributions to the language. I sometimes wonder why I involve myself with this language.

> While on the surface `ScopedValue` seem similar to contextvar in Python it has a subtle but very important difference. The `value` can not change during a scope, only by entering a new dynamic scope the value can be changed. Without this restriction the compiler optimizations could not be implemented.

I don't see how this relates to whether this feature can be described as "dynamic scoping".

> That is a fundamental misunderstanding of the terms variable and values. Julia does not (an likely never will) implement dynamically scoped variables. Variables only have lexical scope to consider.

I understand what variables and values are. The concept of dynamic scoping relates to the binding of *variables*. Your argument might be more convincing if the documentation was careful to use phrases like "dynamically scoped value", rather than claiming that Scoped Values are "an implementation of dynamic scoping in Julia". But as it currently stands, the messaging in the docs is misleading. Here is a list of misleading statements in the manual page:

1. The very first sentence: "Scoped values provide an implementation of dynamic scoping in Julia."
    - `ScopedValue` provides dynamically scoped *values*. It does not provide an implementation of dynamic scoping, which would require dynamically scoped *variables*.
1. "Lexical scoping is the default behavior in Julia."
    - The word "default" implies that Julia does not always use lexical scoping, which is incorrect.
1. "Under dynamic scoping a variable is bound to the most recent assigned value during the program's execution."
    - This statement is correct, but note that it refers to the binding of a *variable*, whereas `ScopedValue` is a dynamically scoped *value*.
1. "Now using a ScopedValue we can use dynamic scoping."
1. "Dynamic scopes are inherited by `Task`s ... In the example below we open a new dynamic scope before launching a task."
1. The `ScopedValue` docstring: "Create a container that propagates values across dynamic scopes. Use `with` to create and enter a new dynamic scope."
    - This is a rather confusing mix of concepts. Dynamic scopes are concerned with the bindings of *variables*, yet here we are talking about propagating *values*.
1. Various other casual references to "dynamic scope" throughout the manual page.

P.S. It would be nice if core developers refrained from using phrases like "That is a fundamental misunderstanding of...". It's remarkable how often I hear that, and usually it's an incorrect assertion. It comes across as condescending and unwelcoming.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1984530877/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644","id":2174448303,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Bm3Kv","number":53644,"title":"Consider renaming `ScopedValue` to `ContextValue`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-03-07T18:04:55Z","updated_at":"2024-03-08T13:26:12Z","closed_at":"2024-03-07T18:50:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Introduction

The new `ScopedValue` feature is really nice, but I think the name and the description of the feature in terms of dynamic scope is likely to lead to confusion.

There was a [claim on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/naming-of-scopedvalues/110747/2?u=cameronbieganek) that the PR had "an extensive analysis and bikeshedding of the naming". However, I have read through the entire PR and I disagree with that statement. There is some discussion of the name, but it is not a very complete discussion. In particular, it is surprising that there was no discussion regarding using the terminology "context" instead of "dynamic scope". In PR #35833, @tkf pointed out that the "context" terminology is used in at least four major programming languages:

- Python: [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html)
- .NET: [`ExecutionContext`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1)
- Kotlin: [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html)
- Go: [`Context`](https://go.dev/blog/context)

Julia is a lexically scoped language, and the Scoped Values PR does not change that. Below are a couple examples that I think emphasize that what `ScopedValue` provides is *not* dynamic scoping.

(Disclaimer: I have only tested these using ScopedValues.jl. I need to install `juliaup` so I can get nightly.)

## Example 1

```julia
julia> using ScopedValues

julia> f() = a[];

julia> function g()
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           f()
       end;

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main ./REPL[2]:1
 [2] g()
   @ Main ./REPL[3]:3
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Example 2

```julia
julia> module A
           f() = a[]
           export f
       end
Main.A

julia> module B
           using ScopedValues
           using ..A
           a = ScopedValue(1)
           g() = f()
           export g
       end
Main.B

julia> using .B

julia> g()
ERROR: UndefVarError: `a` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] f()
   @ Main.A ./REPL[1]:2
 [2] g()
   @ Main.B ./REPL[2]:5
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

## Discussion

Both of these examples would work if `ScopedValue` provided true dynamic scoping. So essentially we are telling the user that Julia uses a subtle combination of lexical scoping and dynamic scoping. I think that's more confusing than it needs to be. We should describe this feature as a "context" feature rather than a "dynamic scoping" feature. This is about more than just the name, it is about our semantic understanding of what the feature provides.

So, after a renaming, this feature could look like this:

```julia
f() = a[]
a = ContextValue(1)

newcontext(a => 2) do
    f()
end # returns 2
```

Of course the precise naming is up for debate. Possible names for the context entering function:

- `with`
- `withcontext`
- `context`
- `incontext`
- `updatecontext`
- `newcontext`

I think `updatecontext` and `newcontext` are probably the most accurate names to describe what they do.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":2,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1985693284","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644#issuecomment-1985693284","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53644","id":1985693284,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM52W0Zk","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-03-08T13:26:11Z","updated_at":"2024-03-08T13:26:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@vchuravy and related functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-02-02T13:10:07Z","updated_at":"2024-02-13T19:25:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53159","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53159.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements `isfull(c::Channel)`. 

It calls `n_avail(c) ≥ c.sz_max` in all cases. The original implementation was inspired by [this comment](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/function-to-check-if-channel-is-full/44795/3?u=thelatekronos), and therefore had a special case for unbuffered channels, which fell back to `isready`. I opted against this behaviour, because it fails to respect that an unbuffered channel is always full, in two important senses:
    1) The number of elements available is greater than or equal the capacity
    2) A call to `put!` will block

With the current implementation, the behaviour is simply understood and summarized in all cases by the start of the docstring:
> Determines whether a `Channel` is full, in the 
sense that calling `put!(c, some_value)` will block.

Shoutout to @SamuraiAku for their work in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/40720, which helped me a lot on thinking this through, and remembering to change all relevant files. In particular, the detail around how `c.cond_take.waitq` may result in immediate unblocking, which is a really important caveat on a function that may be used to check if `put!`ing will block. However, for buffered channels, `isfull` is extremely close to `putwillblock` from #40720 (just a little better, with >= instead of ==), and for unbuffered channels it does not make much sense to see if `put!`ing will block.

This PR is created based on [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22863#issuecomment-1921498544) "call to action".

Checklist:
- [x] Entry in news
- [x] Docstring with example
- [x] Export function
- [x] Mention in manual
- [x] Entry in {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","id":985383893,"node_id":"MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NzI0Njg2NTQw","number":42080,"title":"recommend against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This change adds or pertains to documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":94,"created_at":"2021-09-01T17:00:06Z","updated_at":"2024-02-21T18:43:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42080","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

will probably get closed as a duplicate, but I think it is high time that we provide actual interface definitions for the various collection categories listed on the ["Collections and Data Structures"](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/) page of the manual. That page hints at interfaces for the various categories, but does not provide a clear guide to which methods are required and which methods are optional in order to implement the various interfaces. (Plus, that page includes docstrings for a bunch of functions that most likely do *not* need to be implemented by new types.)

Here is a list of collection interfaces from that page that need better documentation on the interface requirements:

- [ ] General Collections
- [ ] Indexable Collections
- [ ] Dictionaries (`AbstractDict`)
- [ ] Set-Like Collections (`AbstractSet`)
- [ ] Dequeues

See also #34677 and #25941. And also #28784 for documentation of the `AbstractString` interface.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Here's a *sketch* of an implementation:

```julia
function streaming_mean(itr)
    a, rest = Iterators.peel(itr)
    for (i, x) in enumerate(rest)
        a += (x - a) / (i + 1)
    end
    return a
end
```

And here's a demonstration of the numerical performance:

```julia
julia> using Statistics

julia> A = rand(Float32, 100_000_000);

julia> itr = (rand(Float32) for _ in 1:100_000_000);

julia> mean(A), mean(itr), streaming_mean(itr)
(0.4999884f0, 0.16777216f0, 0.49996638f0)
```

(I would imagine that there are some tweaks to be made to optimize the performance of `streaming_mean`, but I hope by now we've learned that correctness (e.g. numerical accuracy) is more important than performance.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52365/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52365/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

> is non-deterministic because it is a generator that calls rand to produce values.

> 
> nor do I see a need, good reason, for rand, but if I'm looking at the right place shouldn't it be documented (if/when user-visible consequences).

It's nothing in particular about generators, it's just that I put `rand()` inside the generator, so every time you iterate `itr`, it generates a new sequence of random numbers. It's not that much different from repeatedly calling `rand(100)`: you get a different sequence of random numbers every time.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1836839319/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

[old comment](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Statistics.jl/pull/34#issuecomment-622062726) from Keno says that "iterators don't in general have a strong guarantee over their ordering". This seems like a large gap in our documentation. Looking at the [docstring for `UnitRange`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1.11-dev/base/collections/#Base.UnitRange), there is no mention of the iteration order. So the following code is undefined:

```julia
x, y = 1:2
```

There's no guarantee that `x == 1` and `y == 2`.

I propose that we update the docstrings for the various iterators in Base to make it clear which iterators have a well-defined iteration order. DataFramesMeta.jl, the data analysis package I maintain, currently exports our own `@with` and will have to re-name the macro before the Julia's v1.11 release. 

`@with` is a very valuable name. It's short and clear. Considering this, having it's use-case for `ScopedValue`s only seems unnecessarily niche to me. 

Would it be possible to re-name the `with` and `@with` to something that doesn't use such a short name? 

I'm not opposed to Julia Base taking over `with` and `@with` in the abstract. But this seems like such a unique use-case that the costs don't outweigh the benefits. ```julia
module A
using B: foo, bar
# ...
end
```

But is that even possible with Base? To my understanding, there is an implicit `using Base` when the REPL is started, so every exported name in Base is brought into scope. If that's the case, than the above pattern cannot protect a package from breakage when Base adds new names. Thus, Base should be cautious about adding very generic names like `@with`, especially if the name is more generic than the actual operation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1856891195/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540","id":2042659537,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM55wILR","number":52540,"title":"Add a mechanism for avoiding package breakage when Base adds new names in a minor release","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-12-14T23:35:11Z","updated_at":"2023-12-14T23:35:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This subject has come up before, most recently in #52535, but I am not aware of an issue that tracks this.

When Base adds new names in a minor release, packages that were already exporting those names become broken. Here's a MWE where the thought experiment is that A exports a function `length`, and then later Base adds and exports a function `length`:

```julia
julia> module A
           length(x) = 42
           export length
       end
Main.A

julia> using .A

julia> length([1, 2])
WARNING: both A and Base export "length"; uses of it in module Main must be qualified
ERROR: UndefVarError: `length` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[3]:1
```

When Package A depends on package B, it can protect itself from breakage due to new names in B by only importing the names it uses from B, like `using B: foo, bar`. However, there is not such mechanism for Base, since there is an implicit `using Base` at the beginning of a REPL session.

We need some mechanism for preventing package breakage when Base adds a new exported name in a minor release. I'm sure I'll get blowback for this, but as it stands right now one of the following two things must happen in order for Julia and its ecosystem to truly follow semantic versioning:

1. Julia should stop adding names in minor releases, because it is a breaking change for any package that already exports that name.
2. Packages should upper bound the version of Julia, because any minor release of Julia could break their package.

Don't shoot the messenger. subject has come up before, most recently in #52535, but I am not aware of an issue that tracks this.

When Base adds new names in a minor release, packages that were already exporting those names become broken. Here's a MWE where the thought experiment is that A exports a function `length`, and then later Base adds and exports a function `length`:

```julia
julia> module A
           length(x) = 42
           export length
       end
Main.A

julia> using .A

julia> length([1, 2])
WARNING: both A and Base export "length"; uses of it in module Main must be qualified
ERROR: UndefVarError: `length` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[3]:1
```

When Package A depends on package B, it can protect itself from breakage due to new names in B by only importing the names it uses from B, like `using B: foo, bar`. However, there is not such mechanism for Base, since there is an implicit `using Base` at the beginning of a REPL session.

We need some mechanism for preventing package breakage when Base adds a new exported name in a minor release. I'm sure I'll get blowback for this, but as it stands right now one of the following two things must happen in order for Julia and its ecosystem to truly follow semantic versioning:

1. Base Julia should stop adding names in minor releases, because it is a breaking change for any package that already exports that name.
2. Packages should upper bound the version of Julia, because any minor release of Julia could break their package.

Don't shoot the messenger. I'm just enumerating what the rules of semantic versioning require. 😅 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1857011757","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540#issuecomment-1857011757","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540","id":1857011757,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ur8At","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-14T23:55:25Z","updated_at":"2023-12-14T23:55:25Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"There is no such requirement on users. Users are not required to follow semantic versioning. It is allowed for users to write a script like this:

```julia
using A
length([1, 2])
```

Suppose we are using version 1.0 of package A, and the compat section of the Project.toml file for version 1.0 of package A contains this entry:

```
julia = "1.6"
```

Then package A is declaring that version 1.0 of package A will work for all versions of Julia greater than or equal to 1.6 and less than 2.0. But suppose Julia 1.7 introduces and exports `length`. Then version 1.0 of package A will be broken on Julia 1.7, even though the compat section declares that it should work.

In other words, this unit test for version 1.0 of package A will pass on (the hypothetical) Julia 1.6 and fail on Julia 1.7:

```julia
using A
using Test
@test length([1, 2]) == 42
```

To reiterate, end user code is under no requirement to use semantic versioning. It is 100% allowed to write `using A` and expect that the code will work if the package manager has installed a compatible set of packages.

> See https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.

Thanks---I've read that issue many times already. It does not address the issue with Base adding new names.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1857011757/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

DataFramesMeta.jl, the data analysis package I maintain, currently exports our own `@with` and will have to re-name the macro before the Julia's v1.11 release. 

`@with` is a very valuable name. It's short and clear. Considering this, having it's use-case for `ScopedValue`s only seems unnecessarily niche to me. 

Would it be possible to re-name the `with` and `@with` to something that doesn't use such a short name? 

I'm not opposed to Julia Base taking over `with` and `@with` in the abstract. But this seems like such a unique use-case that the costs don't outweigh the benefits. ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1857077244","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535#issuecomment-1857077244","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535","id":1857077244,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5usL_8","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-15T00:26:16Z","updated_at":"2023-12-15T00:26:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> In a fresh REPL session:
> 
> ```julia
> julia> using DataFramesMeta: @with
> ```

That's not the package protecting itself from breakage, that's the *package user* protecting themselves from breakage. The current status quo is that `using DataFramesMeta` is allowed (even encouraged by the Julia manual). And if you have a compatible set of packages installed by the package manager, e.g. DataFramesMeta version 0.14.1 on Julia version 1.11, then the following should work, because supposedly the package manager installed a *compatible set of packages*:

```julia
using DataFrames, DataFramesMeta
df = DataFrame(x = 1:2);
@with(df, :x .+ 1)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1857077244/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

subject has come up before, most recently in #52535, but I am not aware of an issue that tracks this.

When Base adds new names in a minor release, packages that were already exporting those names become broken. Here's a MWE where the thought experiment is that A exports a function `length`, and then later Base adds and exports a function `length`:

```julia
julia> module A
           length(x) = 42
           export length
       end
Main.A

julia> using .A

julia> length([1, 2])
WARNING: both A and Base export "length"; uses of it in module Main must be qualified
ERROR: UndefVarError: `length` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[3]:1
```

When Package A depends on package B, it can protect itself from breakage due to new names in B by only importing the names it uses from B, like `using B: foo, bar`. However, there is not such mechanism for Base, since there is an implicit `using Base` at the beginning of a REPL session.

We need some mechanism for preventing package breakage when Base adds a new exported name in a minor release. I'm sure I'll get blowback for this, but as it stands right now one of the following two things must happen in order for Julia and its ecosystem to truly follow semantic versioning:

1. Base Julia should stop adding names in minor releases, because it is a breaking change for any package that already exports that name.
2. Packages should upper bound the version of Julia, because any minor release of Julia could break their package.

Don't shoot the messenger. I'm just enumerating what the rules of semantic versioning require. 😅 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1857338515","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540#issuecomment-1857338515","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52540","id":1857338515,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5utLyT","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-15T06:16:24Z","updated_at":"2023-12-15T06:16:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@LilithHafner made this suggestion in a [comment](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080#issuecomment-1745706532) on the "recommend against `using Foo` PR":

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":90,"created_at":"2021-09-01T17:00:06Z","updated_at":"2023-12-15T13:36:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42080","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

`Base.step` claims the very generic word `step` for a not very generic operation:

```julia
help?> step

  step(r)

  Get the step size of an AbstractRange object.
```

I propose changing the name to `Base.stepsize` for version 2.0. It’s more specific to what the function actually means. Additionally, `stepsize` is clearly a noun rather than a verb (`step` could be either), which indicates that you’re dealing with an accessor function.

I realize this might fall under the category of "it's not important enough to be worth a breaking change", but it's at least worth considering.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40466/reactions","total_count":9,"+1":9,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40466/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1858865099","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40466#issuecomment-1858865099","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40466","id":1858865099,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5uzAfL","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-16T16:45:36Z","updated_at":"2023-12-16T16:45:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"A better name might be `rangestep`. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535","id":2042306910,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM55uyFe","number":52535,"title":"Consider renaming `@with` for ScopedValues","user":{"login":"pdeffebach","id":23196228,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTk2MjI4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23196228?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach","html_url":"https://github.com/pdeffebach","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pdeffebach/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":16,"created_at":"2023-12-14T19:07:19Z","updated_at":"2023-12-17T16:48:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"PR #50958 introduces the `@with` macro along with dynamic scoping generally. 

DataFramesMeta.jl, the data analysis package I maintain, currently exports our own `@with` and will have to re-name the macro before the Julia's v1.11 release. 

`@with` is a very valuable name. It's short and clear. Considering this, having it's use-case for `ScopedValue`s only seems unnecessarily niche to me. 

Would it be possible to re-name the `with` and `@with` to something that doesn't use such a short name? 

I'm not opposed to Julia Base taking over `with` and `@with` in the abstract. But this seems like such a unique use-case that the costs don't outweigh the benefits. ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1859221568","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535#issuecomment-1859221568","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52535","id":1859221568,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5u0XhA","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-12-17T16:48:30Z","updated_at":"2023-12-17T16:48:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> If package A depends on package B, then to avoid breakage when B adds new functions, package A should use the following pattern:
> 
> ```julia
> module A
> using B: foo, bar
> # ...
> end
> ```
> 
> But is that even possible with Base?

Disregard this. I had a misunderstanding about how name ambiguities occur. The problem arises with code like `using A; using B; foo()`, which could occur in a script or inside a package. (And the problem also affects code like `using A; foo()` due to the implicit `using Base` at the beginning.)

That being said, I still think that Base should be cautious about adding new names. And new names should be added at the appropriate level of genericness. In my opinion, the name `with` is too generic for this use case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1859221568/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

inside the function assigns into the parent scope; unpacking in the arglist binds a new variable. ```jl
let x = 1
    function f(θ)
        (;x) = θ
    end

    θ₀ = (;x=2)
    @show x # 1
    f(θ₀)
    @show x # 2
end

let x = 1
    function f((;x))        
    end

    θ₀ = (;x=2)
    @show x # 1
    f(θ₀)
    @show x # 1
end
```

That's on 1.9.1.

@CameronBieganek says both give `1` then `2` on 1.8.2, implying the behavior changed.

I'm not sure what's supposed to happen here.

```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.8.2"

julia> let
           a = 1
           function f((a,)) end
           f((2,))
           a
       end
1

julia> let
           a = 1
           function f((; a)) end
           f((; a=2))
           a
       end
2
```

But in Julia 1.9 they are consistent:

```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.9.3"

julia> let
           a = 1
           function f((a,)) end
           f((2,))
           a
       end
1

julia> let
           a = 1
           function f((; a)) end
           f((; a=2))
           a
       end

1
```

So, this change is probably a good thing. The manual might need to be tweaked, though. Right now it's a little ambiguous, in particular this part:

> If a function argument name is written as a tuple (e.g. `(x, y)`) instead of just a symbol, then an assignment `(x, y) = argument` will be inserted for you.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1709329892/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

is equivalent to this:

In that case, the following sentence would technically be sufficient:

```
The order in which the collection is iterated is undefined.
```

Then it follows that `map!(sqrt, x, x)` produces the correct result, since the iteration order doesn't matter for that call. It also follows that weird aliasing situations might produce incorrect results, but it's not quite obvious from that sentence alone.

...On a previous iteration of this PR, we had the following:

```
The order in which the `collection`s are iterated is undefined. If `destination` shares memory
with any of the `collection`s, the result might be incorrect.
```

That's close, because of the word "might", but still not ideal, because we want the correctness of `map!(sqrt, x, x)` to follow from the docstring, which it does if the only sentence is "The order in which the collection is iterated is
