- [ ] `unsafe_store!`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50824/reactions","total_count":13,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":13,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50824/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741388581","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50824#issuecomment-1741388581","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50824","id":1741388581,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ny3sl","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-29T19:32:40Z","updated_at":"2023-09-29T19:32:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think it is fair to say that if the docstring for a mutating function does not provide a warning on aliasing, that implies that it is safe to use that function when aliasing is present. And that's perfectly fine. Any mutating function that behaves badly when aliasing is present should have a warning in the docstring. That is exactly the issue we are trying to address in this PR. Thus, if this PR adds warnings to all the functions that needs warnings, then we are golden.

Consider two functions `foo!` and `bar!`. Suppose `bar!` has a warning about aliasing and `foo!` does not. It is not the presence of a warning in the `bar!` docstring that implies `foo!` is safe to use when there is aliasing. It is the *absence* of a warning in the `foo!` docstring that implies `foo!` is safe to use. In other words, it is the `foo!` docstring that defines the behavior of `foo!`, not the `bar!` docstring. So, I feel that the worry about one docstring affecting another docstring is misplaced.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741388581/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'd be interested to see examples where aliasing produces incorrect results for `push!` and `append!`.

Also, how about `get!(collection, key, default)`? Off the top of my head, I don't see how aliasing would affect that method. I think are probably plenty of mutating functions with multiple arguments where aliasing is not an issue, so it's not a good idea to issue a blanket statement warning against aliasing in *every single* mutating function with multiple arguments.

I can even make trivial examples where aliasing is not an issue, like this:

```julia
function foo!(x, v)
    @show v
    x[1] = 100
    x
end
```

I think a blanket statement is too alarmist, and often wrong.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741586143/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
julia> d = Dict()
Dict{Any, Any}()

julia> d[2] = d
Dict{Any, Any} with 1 entry:
  2 => Dict{Any, Any}(#= circular reference @-1 =#)

julia> d[d]
ERROR: StackOverflowError:
Stacktrace:
     [1] hash(a::Dict{Any, Any}, h::UInt64)
       @ Base ./abstractdict.jl:534
     [2] hash(x::Dict{Any, Any})
       @ Base ./hashing.jl:28
--- the last 2 lines are repeated 18691 more times ---
 [37385] hash(a::Dict{Any, Any}, h::UInt64)
       @ Base ./abstractdict.jl:534
 [37386] hash
       @ ./hashing.jl:28 [inlined]
 [37387] hashindex
       @ ./dict.jl:157 [inlined]
 [37388] ht_keyindex(h::Dict{Any, Any}, key::Dict{Any, Any})
       @ Base ./dict.jl:266
```

The `push!` example is a bit contrived. `Base.resize!` is not part of the `AbstractArray` interface, according to the interface page of the manual. If you don't extend `resize!` then you get a method error. Also, Base arguably has a bug, because the `push!(a::AbstractVector, iter...)` method eventually calls `resize!`, even though `resize!` is not defined for arbitrary `AbstractVector`.

But I'll give you the `append!` example.

> "if there's aliasing then it's UB"

The problem with this statement is that it's not always true. Here is a simple example of aliased inputs to `push!` where the output is correct (and dare I say, "defined"):

```julia
julia> x = [[1], [2]];

julia> a = x[1];

julia> push!(x, a)
3-element Vector{Vector{Int64}}:
 [1]
 [2]
 [1]
```

And there's my trivial example from my previous post:

```julia
function foo!(x, v)
    @show v
    x[1] = 100
    x
end
```

There are an infinite number of examples like `foo!` where when aliasing is present the behavior is still *defined* and *correct*. So it makes no sense to say that all mutating functions with aliased inputs have undefined behavior. It has to be determined on a case by case basis, and hence it has to go in the function docstrings. If we make a blanket statement, we will be wrong.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741686633/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

That is pretty good, though as you say it's not perfect. There are a few cases where the behavior is (or should be) defined, like `map!(f, x, x)`. (There is a unit test for that case.)

Maybe a slight tweak:

"Unless otherwise noted in the function docstring, it is the responsibility of the caller to ensure that a function behaves properly on aliased inputs. The lack of an explicit warning is not sufficient."

That way `map!` can provide explicit documentation indicating that `map!(f, x, x)` is a supported idiom. I'm not sure what we would need to add to the `push!` docstring to indicate that simple aliased cases like `x = [[1], [2]]; a = x[1]; push!(x, a)` are intended to work.

The alternative is to give the various mutating functions more precise definitions that somehow specify the order of operations, and then the behavior is no longer undefined. That's what I suggested for `map!` in PR #47012. (I haven't studied all the relevant functions to determine how plausible this approach is.)

This whole discussion makes me want to switch to a language with less rampant mutation everywhere. :joy:","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741775296/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I thought it looked like aliasing, but I'm not a computer scientist, so maybe not? I suppose `x` is backed in memory by two pointers, and `a` is backed in memory by the array to which the first pointer in `x` points, so the memory does not overlap.

As far as I know, Julia does not have a well-defined memory model, so it might be difficult to accurately define aliasing from within the language.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1741875219/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

However, I'm not sure what to do with `map!`. I believe the `map!(f, x, x)` idiom is meant to be supported, e.g., the following should work:

```julia
julia> x = [1, 2];

julia> map!(x -> 2x, x, x); x
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 2
 4
```

In fact, there is a [unit test](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/8b5e3e910be9db6fa3e462ff1d05f6d355ab103d/test/generic_map_tests.jl#L49) for that use case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1668600386/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857","id":1843577442,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5t4sJi","number":50857,"title":"Define the output order from `map`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-08-09T15:58:49Z","updated_at":"2023-08-09T15:58:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently the `map` docstring makes no assertion about the order of the output collection from `map`. So, `map(x -> 2x, 1:2) == [4, 2]` is conformant with the `map` docstring. Thus, strictly speaking, no one should ever use `map` unless they are going to perform a reduction (with an associative operator) on the output. Here is a proposal for an updated docstring that defines the output order:

```julia
"""
    map(f, x...)

Transform iterable `x` by applying `f` to each element. The order of the output matches the order of the input.  
In other words, the following expression is always true:

all(zip(map(f, xs), xs)) do (fx, x)
    fx == f(x)
end

For multiple collection arguments, apply `f` elementwise, and stop when when any of them is exhausted.
"""
```

Note that I've also switched the terminology from "collection" to "iterable", since `map` works on arbitrary iterable objects which are not necessarily collections. For example, `length` throws a `MethodError` when called on an iterator like

```julia
itr = (sin(x) for x in 1:10 if sin(x) > 0)
```

and the [manual implies](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#General-Collections) that `length` must be implemented for an object to be considered a collection.

The docstring for `zip` is also a bit vague, so in order to adopt the above change to the `map` docstring, we would also need to clarify the `zip` docstring.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

`map`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-08-09T15:58:49Z","updated_at":"2023-08-10T01:17:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently the `map` docstring makes no assertion about the order of the output collection from `map`. So, `map(x -> 2x, 1:2) == [4, 2]` is conformant with the `map` docstring. Thus, strictly speaking, no one should ever use `map` unless they are going to perform a reduction (with an associative operator) on the output. Here is a proposal for an updated docstring that defines the output order:

```julia
"""
    map(f, x...)

Transform iterable `x` by applying `f` to each element. The order of the output matches the order of the input.  
In other words, the following expression is always true:

all(zip(map(f, xs), xs)) do (fx, x)
    fx == f(x)
end

For multiple collection arguments, apply `f` elementwise, and stop when when any of them is exhausted.
"""
```

Note that I've also switched the terminology from "collection" to "iterable", since `map` works on arbitrary iterable objects which are not necessarily collections. For example, `length` throws a `MethodError` when called on an iterator like

```julia
itr = (sin(x) for x in 1:10 if sin(x) > 0)
```

and the [manual implies](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#General-Collections) that `length` must be implemented for an object to be considered a collection.

The docstring for `zip` is also a bit vague, so in order to adopt the above change to the `map` docstring, we would also need to clarify the `zip` docstring.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1672392233","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857#issuecomment-1672392233","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50857","id":1672392233,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5jrq4p","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-10T01:17:58Z","updated_at":"2023-08-10T01:17:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I thought about stateful iterators a while ago, but I finally got around to opening the issue today. So I forgot to add a `copy` to handle stateful iterators. Regarding non-Bool values, I suppose the comparison should be with `isequal`. So, combining those two changes, we have

```julia
all(zip(map(f, copy(xs)), xs)) do (fx, x)
    isequal(fx, f(x))
end
```

Impure functions seem harder to handle. The following works, but it feels like it works for the wrong reasons:

```julia
julia> f(x) = push!(x, 10)
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> xs = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
2-element Vector{Vector{Int64}}:
 [1, 2]
 [3, 4]

julia> all(zip(map(f, copy(xs)), xs)) do (fx, x)
           a = f(x)
           @show a
           @show fx
           isequal(fx, a)
       end
a = [1, 2, 10, 10]
fx = [1, 2, 10, 10]
a = [3, 4, 10, 10]
fx = [3, 4, 10, 10]
true
```

There's probably a counter-example with an impure function where the `all` expression returns `false`...

At any rate, the `all` expression is starting to look a bit too unwieldy to add to the docstring. Perhaps there's a better way to describe the output order in prose. The best I can think of at the moment is something like this:

```
The order of the output matches the order of the input. For example,
the first element of the output is `f(first(x))` and the last element of the
output is `f(last(x))`.
```

> And yeah, the order of `zip` is actually [not guaranteed](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47790#issuecomment-1342224650) either.

I think that comment is referring to the fact that, for `zip(itr1, itr2)`, it is undefined whether the $i$-th tuple is evaluated by first iterating `itr1` and then iterating `itr2` or by first iterating `itr2` and then iterating `itr1`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1672392233/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

       elimination example with automatic tuning of the number of features
       selected with cross-validation.
 
+Recursive Feature Elimination with Cross-Validation (RFECV)
+============================================================
+
+RFECV is a feature selection technique that aims to find an optimal subset of features by iteratively eliminating the least important features in a cross-validation loop. This process helps to balance model complexity and performance, ensuring selected features contribute meaningfully to the model's predictive power.
+
+Key Steps of RFECV
+
+   - **Initialization**: Begin with all available features from the dataset.
+
+   - **Iteration**:
+       - **Feature Importance Estimation**: Train an estimator on the current feature subset and assess feature importance. Importance is determined based on coefficients, feature importances, or a provided callable function.
+       - **Cross-Validation**: Evaluate estimator performance using cross-validation on the current feature subset.
+
+    - **Feature Elimination**: Remove the least important features based on the results of the previous step.
+
+    - **Convergence Check**: Continue iterating until a stopping criterion is met, such as reaching a specified feature count or desired performance threshold.
+
+    - **Optimal Subset Selection**: At the end of iteration, select the feature subset with the highest cross-validated performance. This subset is accessible through the `support_` attribute of the `RFECV` object.","path":"doc/modules/feature_selection.rst","commit_id":"8dfcfb5a1ea5aa4e695ab82ca85634cb4d621a61","original_commit_id":"8dfcfb5a1ea5aa4e695ab82ca85634cb4d621a61","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This seems like a re-wording of the conjecture that I made in #27193. Fixes #27159 
Fixes #27193 


#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
This commit adds information about common splitting strategies in decision trees, enhancing the documentation for decision tree construction. Specifically, it introduces details about the "Best Split Strategy" and the "Random Split Strategy" commonly used in decision tree algorithms. The content explains how these strategies work, their impact on tree quality and computational complexity, and when to choose one over the other. The documentation is aimed at providing users a better understanding of decision tree construction strategies, enabling them to make informed choices when using decision tree algorithms. 
The second commit enhances the scikit-learn documentation by introducing a comprehensive example and explanation of Recursive Feature Elimination with Cross-Validation (RFECV). The provided example demonstrates the practical application of RFECV in a classification scenario with synthetic data, encompassing informative, redundant, and non-informative features.

#### Reference Issues/PRs
<!--
Example: Fixes #1234. See also #3456.
Please use keywords (e.g., Fixes) to create link to the issues or pull requests
you resolved, so that they will automatically be closed when your pull request
is merged. See https://github.com/blog/1506-closing-issues-via-pull-requests
-->
Fixes #27159 
Fixes #27193 


#### What does this implement/fix? Explain your changes.
This commit adds information about common splitting strategies in decision trees, enhancing the documentation for decision tree construction. Specifically, it introduces details about the "Best Split Strategy" and the "Random Split Strategy" commonly used in decision tree algorithms. The content explains how these strategies work, their impact on tree quality and computational complexity, and when to choose one over the other. The documentation is aimed at providing users a better understanding of decision tree construction strategies, enabling them to make informed choices when using decision tree algorithms. 
The second commit enhances the scikit-learn documentation by introducing a comprehensive example and explanation of Recursive Feature Elimination with Cross-Validation (RFECV). The provided example demonstrates the practical application of RFECV in a classification scenario with synthetic data, encompassing informative, redundant, and non-informative features.

#### Any other comments?

Should I expect that from something in https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/variables-and-scoping/ or is that arglist-unpacking a special behavior? 

```jl
let x = 1
    function f(θ)
        (;x) = θ
    end

    θ₀ = (;x=2)
    @show x # 1
    f(θ₀)
    @show x # 2
end

let x = 1
    function f((;x))        
    end

    θ₀ = (;x=2)
    @show x # 1
    f(θ₀)
    @show x # 1
end
```

That's on 1.9.1.

@CameronBieganek says both give `1` then `2` on 1.8.2, implying the behavior changed.

I'm not sure what's supposed to happen here.


[Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/unpacking-in-arglist-vs-inside-function/103264).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51107/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51107/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1699298343","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51107#issuecomment-1699298343","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51107","id":1699298343,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5lSTwn","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-30T14:30:30Z","updated_at":"2023-08-30T14:30:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Seems like a bug to me. that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-10-02T16:36:35Z","updated_at":"2023-04-04T14:23:04Z","closed_at":"2023-03-30T03:33:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47012","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fixes #46352.

A consensus has not been reached in #46352, so this represents my opinion on the correct resolution of that issue. Basically, my resolution is to declare the existing behavior as correct and assert that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`. Given the possibility of aliasing, and given that `map!` is intended to act in-place on the destination collection, I think this is the only feasible definition.

I added the unit tests outside of the `generic_map_tests()` function, because I don't think the "iteratively apply `f`" definition would make sense for `asyncmap!`. Please correct me if I am wrong. There appears to be an effort to have the various mapping functions have as similar a behavior as possible, but they are different functions after all, so I think it's ok to have some variation in the documented behaviors.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1669479222,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5jgjs2","number":49372,"title":"Should `allequal` use `==` instead of `isequal`?","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-04-15T16:42:57Z","updated_at":"2023-04-15T16:42:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When I worked on the PR to add `allequal` (#43353), I used `isequal` instead of `==`, because `unique` and `allunique` use `isequal`. However, in retrospect that seems like the wrong choice. It seems to me that users looking for `allequal` functionality will generally want to compare values with `==`. In particular, it is unfortunate that `allequal` does not follow three-valued logic. So, we currently have the following behavior, which seems undesirable to me:

```julia
julia> allequal([NaN, NaN])
true

julia> allequal([missing, missing])
true

julia> allequal([1, missing])
false
```

I think for most use cases it would be better for those to return `false`, `missing`, and `missing`, respectively.

In the PR discussion, I [mentioned the difference](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43354#issuecomment-988359411) between using `isequal` and using `==`, but nobody objected to using `isequal`. 😅 

Unfortunately this would be a breaking change. I would almost consider the failure to propagate missingness a bug, but technically it's not a bug since the docstring does say that comparisons are made using `isequal`.

Would this qualify as one of those "technically breaking" changes that we could slip into a minor release?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1669479222,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5jgjs2","number":49372,"title":"Should `allequal` use `==` instead of `isequal`?","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-04-15T16:42:57Z","updated_at":"2023-04-15T19:15:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When I worked on the PR to add `allequal` (#43353), I used `isequal` instead of `==`, because `unique` and `allunique` use `isequal`. However, in retrospect that seems like the wrong choice. It seems to me that users looking for `allequal` functionality will generally want to compare values with `==`. In particular, it is unfortunate that `allequal` does not follow three-valued logic. So, we currently have the following behavior, which seems undesirable to me:

```julia
julia> allequal([NaN, NaN])
true

julia> allequal([missing, missing])
true

julia> allequal([1, missing])
false
```

I think for most use cases it would be better for those to return `false`, `missing`, and `missing`, respectively.

In the PR discussion, I [mentioned the difference](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43354#issuecomment-988359411) between using `isequal` and using `==`, but nobody objected to using `isequal`. 😅 

Unfortunately this would be a breaking change. I would almost consider the failure to propagate missingness a bug, but technically it's not a bug since the docstring does say that comparisons are made using `isequal`.

Would this qualify as one of those "technically breaking" changes that we could slip into a minor release?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1509934866","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372#issuecomment-1509934866","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1509934866,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Z_8cS","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-15T19:15:13Z","updated_at":"2023-04-15T19:15:13Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I don't have a particular use case in mind. I just have a gut feeling that comparison with `==` is more generally useful, especially since it propagates missingness.

Here's an example inspired by a [question on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-do-i-get-the-indexes-for-matrix-columns-whose-elements-are-all-the-same/97402). Suppose we have the following matrix:

```julia
m = [1        missing  NaN  3  4
     missing  missing  NaN  3  5]
```

And then we want to call `findall(allequal, eachcol(m))`. The problem is, there is no obvious right way to do it. It depends on what the user is looking for. We probably shouldn't have added `allequal`, because it's hard to predict which version of equality the user is looking for, whether it be `==`, `isequal`, or something in between, like

```julia
function myequals(x, y)
    isnan(x) && isnan(y) && return true
    x == y
end
``` 

When I opened the [issue](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43353) and [the PR](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43354), there were some objections to expanding the Base namespace, but I don't think anyone brought up the issue of ambiguity over the type of equality comparison to use.

isequal"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-04-15T16:42:57Z","updated_at":"2023-04-17T13:35:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When I worked on the PR to add `allequal` (#43353), I used `isequal` instead of `==`, because `unique` and `allunique` use `isequal`. However, in retrospect that seems like the wrong choice. It seems to me that users looking for `allequal` functionality will generally want to compare values with `==`. In particular, it is unfortunate that `allequal` does not follow three-valued logic. So, we currently have the following behavior, which seems undesirable to me:

```julia
julia> allequal([NaN, NaN])
true

julia> allequal([missing, missing])
true

julia> allequal([1, missing])
false
```

I think for most use cases it would be better for those to return `false`, `missing`, and `missing`, respectively.

In the PR discussion, I [mentioned the difference](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/43354#issuecomment-988359411) between using `isequal` and using `==`, but nobody objected to using `isequal`. 😅 

Unfortunately this would be a breaking change. I would almost consider the failure to propagate missingness a bug, but technically it's not a bug since the docstring does say that comparisons are made using `isequal`.

Would this qualify as one of those "technically breaking" changes that we could slip into a minor release?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1511362202","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372#issuecomment-1511362202","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49372","id":1511362202,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5aFY6a","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-17T13:35:59Z","updated_at":"2023-04-17T13:35:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> First of all, it would be extremely weird if a function named `allequal` used something other than `isequal` for comparisons.

`all==` is not a syntactically valid identifier, so what else could `all(==(first(A)), A)` possibly be named? The `==` symbol is read as "equals", after all. Also, many beginner Julia programmers are not even aware of `isequal`.

> Next is probably a matter of opinion, but would be nice not to pollute more and more fundamentally boolean function results with missings.

`all` itself already propagates `missing`, so it shouldn't be too surprising if `allequal` also propagates missing.

```julia
julia> all([true, missing])
missing
```

Once you buy in to three-valued logic at the language level, it's hard to justify *not* using three-valued logic.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1511362202/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49095","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49095/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49095/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49095/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49095","id":1634944335,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5hc0VP","number":49095,"title":"Change `Dict` field names to `_keys` and `_vals`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-03-22T02:07:10Z","updated_at":"2023-03-22T13:45:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"To avoid new users accidentally accessing the internals of a dictionary via `d.keys` or `d.vals` (e.g., if they're coming from an OOP language), it's probably a good idea to change the names of those fields to `_keys` and `_vals`.

stacktraces by holding non-`nothing` optional values in a named tuple","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-03-23T23:15:32Z","updated_at":"2023-03-23T23:15:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As [discussed on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/differentialequations-package-kills-performance-everywhere-when-truncatedstacktraces-is-used/96417/29?u=cameronbieganek), it is possible to improve the SciML stacktraces by storing non-`nothing` optional values in a named tuple, rather than having highly parameterized structs. Since most of the optional fields in SciML structs end up being set to nothing, using a small named tuple with the non-`nothing` values significantly cuts down on the length of type signatures. This idea applies to any struct that contains many optional fields. Here is an example in the spirit of `ODEFunction`. ```julia struct MyFunction{F,N,T} f::F more_funcs::NamedTuple{N,T} end function MyFunction(f; kwargs...) # Extract the named tuple from the Base.Pairs to reduce # the type complexity. MyFunction(f, values(kwargs)) end ``` For comparison, here is a type `SciMLFunction` that is essentially the same as `ODEFunction`. ```julia struct SciMLFunction{iip, specialize, F, TMM, Ta, Tt, TJ, JVP, VJP, JP, SP, TW, TWt, TPJ, S, S2, S3, O, TCV, SYS} f::F mass_matrix::TMM analytic::Ta tgrad::Tt jac::TJ jvp::JVP vjp::VJP jac_prototype::JP sparsity::SP Wfact::TW Wfact_t::TWt paramjac::TPJ syms::S indepsym::S2 paramsyms::S3 observed::O colorvec::TCV sys::SYS end function SciMLFunction(f; mass_matrix=nothing, analytic=nothing, tgrad=nothing, jac=nothing, jvp=nothing, vjp=nothing, jac_prototype=nothing, sparsity=nothing, Wfact=nothing, Wfact_t=nothing, paramjac=nothing, syms=nothing, indepsym=nothing, paramsyms=nothing, observed=nothing, colorvec=nothing, sys=nothing) SciMLFunction{1, 2, typeof(f), typeof(mass_matrix), typeof(analytic), typeof(tgrad), typeof(jac), typeof(jvp), typeof(vjp), typeof(jac_prototype), typeof(sparsity), typeof(Wfact), typeof(Wfact_t), typeof(paramjac), typeof(syms), typeof(indepsym), typeof(paramsyms), typeof(observed), typeof(colorvec), typeof(sys)}(f, mass_matrix, analytic, tgrad, jac, jvp, vjp, jac_prototype, sparsity, Wfact, Wfact_t, paramjac, syms, indepsym, paramsyms, observed, colorvec, sys) end ``` And here are two functions `foo` that operate on a `SciMLFunction` or `MyFunction`. ```julia function foo(funcs::SciMLFunction, x) g = isnothing(funcs.jac) ? identity : funcs.jac h = isnothing(funcs.jvp) ? identity : funcs.jvp k = isnothing(funcs.vjp) ? identity : funcs.vjp funcs.f(g(h(k(x)))) end function foo(funcs::MyFunction, x) g = get(funcs.more_funcs, :jac, identity) h = get(funcs.more_funcs, :jvp, identity) k = get(funcs.more_funcs, :vjp, identity) funcs.f(g(h(k(x)))) end ``` Now let's initialize the data structures: ```julia sciml_funcs = SciMLFunction(x -> x + 1) my_funcs = MyFunction(x -> x + 1) ``` Now let's compare the stacktraces generated by `foo` for the two different types. Here's a screenshot of the stacktrace created by `SciMLFunction`: And here's a screenshot of the stacktrace created by `MyFunction`: If we compare frame 2 from each stacktrace, we see that the printing of the `MyFunction` type is much shorter than the printing of the `SciMLFunction` type. I think it's a considerable improvement. Implementing this technique across the SciML ecosystem has the potential to significantly reduce the size and complexity of SciML stacktraces.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/issues/425/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/issues/425/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"closed","number":47012,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47012","id":1073887209,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5AAjfp","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012","number":47012,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Declare that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Fixes #46352.

A consensus has not been reached in #46352, so this represents my opinion on the correct resolution of that issue. Basically, my resolution is to declare the existing behavior as correct and assert that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`. Given the possibility of aliasing, and given that `map!` is intended to act in-place on the destination collection, I think this is the only feasible definition.

I added the unit tests outside of the `generic_map_tests()` function, because I don't think the "iteratively apply `f`" definition would make sense for `asyncmap!`. Please correct me if I am wrong. There appears to be an effort to have the various mapping functions have as similar a behavior as possible, but they are different functions after all, so I think it's ok to have some variation in the documented behaviors.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","id":1393823784,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5AAjfp","number":47012,"title":"Declare that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-10-02T16:36:35Z","updated_at":"2023-03-30T03:33:05Z","closed_at":"2023-03-30T03:33:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47012","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fixes #46352.

A consensus has not been reached in #46352, so this represents my opinion on the correct resolution of that issue. Basically, my resolution is to declare the existing behavior as correct and assert that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`. Given the possibility of aliasing, and given that `map!` is intended to act in-place on the destination collection, I think this is the only feasible definition.

I added the unit tests outside of the `generic_map_tests()` function, because I don't think the "iteratively apply `f`" definition would make sense for `asyncmap!`. Please correct me if I am wrong. There appears to be an effort to have the various mapping functions have as similar a behavior as possible, but they are different functions after all, so I think it's ok to have some variation in the documented behaviors.

issue","color":"0052cc","default":true,"description":"Indicates a good issue for first-time contributors to Julia"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-05-01T20:46:40Z","updated_at":"2023-02-08T21:05:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I think that every function that handles `missing` values should have its handling of `missing` values documented in its docstring. There are two possible ways to do this:

- Add discussion of the handling of `missing` values to the generic `foo(x)` docstring.
- Add separate docstrings for all the `foo(::Missing)`, `bar(::Missing, ::Any)`, etc, methods.
    - This approach won't work for methods that take iterators, like `all`, `any`, `maximum`, and `minimum`.

Functions that handle missing values currently do not have fully specified APIs, since their docstrings make no mention of `missing` values. My position is that docstrings should always strive to fully specify the API of their functions, so that in theory a person could write an entire application by referring only to the docstrings. super hacky, quick implementation of some ideas from

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fixing-the-piping-chaining-issue

Parse chains of `/>` and `\>` at the rough same precedence as `|>`, but treat them as a "frontfix/backfix operator" for function calls such that the succeeding function call becomes curried in first or last argument. Thus, the following

    x  />  f(y)  \>  g(z)

is parsed as

    (chain x (/> f y) (\> g z))

and lowered to the equivalent of

    chain(x, a->f(a, y), b->g(z, b))

Also add lowering of underscore as strictly tight-binding placeholder syntax. (Super hacky - more forms should be allowed! This is just for experimentation).

--- 

Note, I don't really expect to ever merge this, given how difficult https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990 has proven :grimacing: 

It's just an experiment for people to play with.

You can try this by checking out this branch and using

```
JuliaSyntax.enable_in_core!(freeze_world_age=false)
```

(With this setup, you can also edit JuliaSyntax.jl to play with different lowering scenarios and see them almost immediately in the REPL using Revise.  (May need to run a command twice for Revise to pick things up.))","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/1307510804","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148#issuecomment-1307510804","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148","id":1307510804,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM5N7wgU","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-08T16:43:09Z","updated_at":"2022-11-08T16:43:09Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Is this intended to implement the original proposal in that Discourse thread, or a variation on that theme? super hacky, quick implementation of some ideas from

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fixing-the-piping-chaining-issue

Tiny demo:

```julia
julia> 1:10 \> filter(isodd) \> map(_^2)
5-element Vector{Int64}:
  1
  9
 25
 49
 81
```

---

Implementation: Parse chains of `/>` and `\>` at the rough same precedence as `|>`, treating them as a currying operator for function calls such that the succeeding function call becomes curried with the only free argument as either the first or last, respectively. Thus, the following

    x  />  f(y)  \>  g(z)

is parsed as

    (chain x (/> f y) (\> g z))

and lowered to

    chain(x, fixbutfirst(f, y), fixbutlast(g, z))

Also, this can be used without data on the left hand side to create a first class data pipeline:

    h = />  f(y)  \>  g(z)

Lowers to

    compose_chain(fixbutfirst(f, y), fixbutlast(g, z))

Additionally, add lowering of underscore as strictly tight-binding placeholder syntax. (Super hacky - more forms should be allowed! This is just for experimentation).

--- 

Note, I don't really expect to ever merge this, given how difficult https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990 has proven :grimacing: 

It's just an experiment for people to play with.

You can try this by checking out this branch and using

```
JuliaSyntax.enable_in_core!(freeze_world_age=false)
```

(With this setup, you can also edit JuliaSyntax.jl to play with different lowering scenarios and see them almost immediately in the REPL using Revise.  (May need to run a command twice for Revise to pick things up.))","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":5,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/1309145441","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148#issuecomment-1309145441","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148","id":1309145441,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM5OB_lh","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-09T18:01:08Z","updated_at":"2022-11-09T18:01:08Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Here's my comparison of this PR to the original Discourse proposal:

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fixing-the-piping-chaining-issue/89654/178?u=cameronbieganek

TLDR:
This PR behaves very similarly to having a "front pipe" and a "back pipe". I think it is semantically cleaner to just have a "front pipe" and a "back pipe" than to have "fix every argument but the first" and "fix every argument but the last" with an implicit chain operation thrown in. All that being said, I would still prefer having underscore currying over having front and back pipes. :)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/1309145441/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","id":1393823784,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5AAjfp","number":47012,"title":"Declare that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-10-02T16:36:35Z","updated_at":"2022-11-10T20:46:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47012","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fixes #46352.

A consensus has not been reached in #46352, so this represents my opinion on the correct resolution of that issue. Basically, my resolution is to declare the existing behavior as correct and assert that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`. Given the possibility of aliasing, and given that `map!` is intended to act in-place on the destination collection, I think this is the only feasible definition.

I added the unit tests outside of the `generic_map_tests()` function, because I don't think the "iteratively apply `f`" definition would make sense for `asyncmap!`. Please correct me if I am wrong. There appears to be an effort to have the various mapping functions have as similar a behavior as possible, but they are different functions after all, so I think it's ok to have some variation in the documented behaviors.

Playing with currying, chaining and underscores","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2022-11-08T07:22:03Z","updated_at":"2022-11-10T21:51:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/148","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"A super hacky, quick implementation of "pipefirst / pipelast" ideas inspired by

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fixing-the-piping-chaining-issue

Tiny demo:

```julia
julia> 1:10 \> filter(isodd) \> map(z->z^2)
5-element Vector{Int64}:
  1
  9
 25
 49
 81
```

The meaning of `\>` is roughly "pipelast", so the above expression is like `x |> (y->filter(isodd, y)) |> (y->map(z->z^2, y))` though with different lowering. Similarly `/>` is "pipefirst".

## Implementation

Parse chains of `/>` and `\>` at the rough same precedence as `|>`, treating them as a currying operator for function calls such that the succeeding function call becomes curried with the only free argument as either the first or last, respectively. Thus, the following

    x  />  f(y)  \>  g(z)

is parsed as

    (chain x (/> (call f y)) (\> (call g z)))

and lowered to

    chain(x, fixbutfirst(f, y), fixbutlast(g, z))

Also, this can be used without data on the left hand side to create a first class data pipeline:

    h = />  f(y)  \>  g(z)

Lowers to

    compose_chain(fixbutfirst(f, y), fixbutlast(g, z))

Additionally, add lowering of underscore as strictly tight-binding placeholder syntax. (Super hacky - more forms should be allowed! This is just for experimentation).

--- 

Note, I don't really expect to ever merge this, given how difficult https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990 has proven :grimacing: 

It's just an experiment for people to play with.

You can try this by checking out this branch and using

```
JuliaSyntax.enable_in_core!(freeze_world_age=false)
```

(With this setup, you can also edit JuliaSyntax.jl to play with different lowering scenarios and see them almost immediately in the REPL using Revise.  (May need to run a command twice for Revise to pick things up.))","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":5,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/1310951832","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/148#issuecomment-1310951832","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/148","id":1310951832,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM5OI4mY","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-10T21:51:36Z","updated_at":"2022-11-10T21:51:36Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> So, it turns out that the lowering adopted here can provide fundamentally more efficient data pipelines than normal piping by allowing rewrite rules to be added as methods to `chain`.

One could imagine the underscore currying lowering to a `FixArgs` type. Then perhaps similar things could be done with `FixArgs`. However, I'm not sure if it would actually be possible, since `|>` is just a regular operator/function with no special lowering. So an expression like this,

```julia
x |> map(sqrt, _) |> reduce(+, _)
```

would be equivalent to something like this,

```julia
FixArgs(reduce, (1, ), +)(
    FixArgs(map, (1, ), sqrt)(x)
)
```

so there doesn't seem to be a way to intercept the `FixArgs(map, ...)` and `FixArgs(reduce, ...)` and convert them to a `mapreduce`.

> Transducers are still fundamentally more composable than a pile of rewrite rules. I wonder whether we could get them in here somehow.

Yeah, perhaps transducers are the *real* way to make piping in Base more powerful. (Well, Base piping is already powerful, it's just the syntax with a bunch of anonymous functions that is annoying.) Although it seems that the usage of `|>` in Transducers.jl is kind of a pun. I've only recently started to look into transducers, but my understanding is that the correct way to combine transducers is composition, since a transducer is a function. Transducers in Clojure are combined with `compose`. Of course, with a little digging, I see that Transducers.jl does provide both a `compose` function and an `opcompose` function. I need to do more studying of transducers, both in Clojure and in Transducers.jl.

Anyhow, maybe the moral of the story is that if you use transducers, then you don't even need piping, you just need function composition. It would be cool if in Julia 2.0 `map(f)` and `filter(f)` returned transducers like they do in Clojure.

This may have been filed already, but let me file it here anyway as I couldn't find existing issue:
```julia
julia> function f(c, x)
           local var = nothing
           if c
               var = Any[]
           end
           function usevar(x)
               if var !== nothing
                   push!(var, x)
                   return var
               else
                   return nothing
               end
           end
           return usevar(x)
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> code_lowered(f, (Bool,Int))
1-element Vector{Core.CodeInfo}:
 CodeInfo(
1 ─      var = Core.Box()
│        Core.NewvarNode(:(usevar))
│   %3 = Main.nothing
│        Core.setfield!(var, :contents, %3)
└──      goto #3 if not c
2 ─ %6 = Base.getindex(Main.Any)
└──      Core.setfield!(var, :contents, %6)
3 ┄      usevar = %new(Main.:(var"#usevar#3"), var)
│   %9 = (usevar)(x)
└──      return %9
)
```

I'm willing to look at improving the lowering implementation if anyone kindly tell me which part of our codebase should be modified.

One reason to add `allequal` is that a proper implementation is much faster than the naive `length(unique(itr)) == 1`.

```julia
function allequal(x)
    first, rest = Iterators.peel(x)

    for v in rest
        if v == first
            continue
        else
            return false
        end
    end

    true
end

slow_allequal(x) = length(unique(x)) == 1
```

```julia
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> x = randn(1000); y = fill(1, 1000);

julia> @btime allequal($x);
  3.288 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime slow_allequal($x);
  36.791 μs (27 allocations: 65.95 KiB)

julia> @btime allequal($y);
  475.082 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime slow_allequal($y);
  3.816 μs (6 allocations: 608 bytes)
```

to the [docstring for `map!`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#Base.map!), the destination must be at least as large as the smallest collection. However, this is not enforced for some array types:

```julia
julia> x = zeros(2);

julia> map!(sqrt, x, 1:3)
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 1.0
 1.4142135623730951

julia> map!(+, x, 1:3, 1:3)
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 2.0
 4.0
```
