{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707","id":1369089055,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5RmqQf","number":46707,"title":"`map!` should error if the length of `dest` is less than that of `src`. ","user":{"login":"MasonProtter","id":29157027,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5MTU3MDI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/29157027?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter","html_url":"https://github.com/MasonProtter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MasonProtter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-09-11T22:56:45Z","updated_at":"2022-10-02T14:53:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently,
```julia
julia> map!(identity, [], [1,2,3])
Any[]
```
This is problematic because one might assume incorrectly that it'd `push!` to the `destination` argument. According to the docstring, the `destination` **must** be longer than than the smallest `collection` being mapped over:
```
  Like map, but stores the result in destination rather than a new collection. destination must be at least as large as the smallest collection.
```
I think we could avoid some silent surprises if this errored (as the docstring implies)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1264662052","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707#issuecomment-1264662052","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46707","id":1264662052,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LYTYk","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-02T14:53:45Z","updated_at":"2022-10-02T14:53:45Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Technically this should probably be considered a bug fix, but I wonder if this might break any packages. It definitely seems like the kind of thing that might be used (intentionally or unintentionally) by some package in the wild.

Another example, just to show how naturally it can arise (i.e. not just for empty vectors).

```julia
julia> a = zeros(2);

julia> map!(x -> x + 1, a, 1:3)
2-element Vector{Float64}:
 2.0

{"action":"opened","number":47012,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47012","id":1073887209,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5AAjfp","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47012.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47012","number":47012,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Declare that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Fixes #46352.

A consensus has not been reached in #46352, so this represents my opinion on the correct resolution of that issue. Basically, my resolution is to declare the existing behavior as correct and assert that `f` is applied iteratively in `map!`. Given the possibility of aliasing, and given that `map!` is intended to act in-place on the destination collection, I think this is the only feasible definition.

I added the unit tests outside of the `generic_map_tests()` function, because I don't think the "iteratively apply `f`" definition would make sense for `asyncmap!`. Please correct me if I am wrong. There appears to be an effort to have the various mapping functions have as similar a behavior as possible, but they are different functions after all, so I think it's ok to have some variation in the documented behaviors.

+    # then update bits by `or`ing with a masked RHS
+    destc[len_Ac] |= f(Ac[end]) & _msk

 end

+    @testset "Issue #47011, map! over unequal length bitarray" begin
+        for l = [0, 1, 63, 64, 65, 127, 128, 129, 255, 256, 257, 6399, 6400, 6401]

+
+                b1 = bitrand(l+extra_l)
+                b2 = bitrand(l)
+                original_tail = last(b1, extra_l)
+                for op in (!, ~)
+                    map!(op, b1, b2)
+                    @test first(b1, l) == map(op, b2)
+                    # check we didn't change bits we're not suppose to
+                    @test last(b1, extra_l) == original_tail
+                end
+
+                b3 = bitrand(l)
+                b4 = bitrand(l+extra_len)
+                for op in (|, ⊻)
+                    map!(op, b1, b2, b3)

+                    # check we didn't change bits we're not suppose to
+                    @test last(b1, extra_l) == original_tail
+
+                    map!(op, b1, b2, b4)
+                    @test first(b1, l) == map(op, b2, b4)
+                    # check we didn't change bits we're not suppose to
+                    @test last(b1, extra_l) == original_tail

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996","id":1205792072,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5H3u1I","number":44996,"title":"Add `findfirstvalue(f, itr)`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2022-04-15T18:46:42Z","updated_at":"2022-10-15T15:07:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"_EDIT: Title changed base on feedback from @nlw0._ 

I often find myself wishing that we had a version of `findfirst(f, A)` that just returned the first `x` for which `f(x)` is true, rather than returning the index. Granted, you could just write that as `A[findfirst(f, A)]`, but that's less elegant for long array names, like

```julia
very_long_name[findfirst(f, very_long_name)]
```

Furthermore, `findfirst(f, A)` does not work for arbitrary iterators:

```julia
julia> itr = Iterators.drop(1:8, 4)
Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)

julia> findfirst(isodd, itr)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching keys(::Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}})
```

We do have the following option,

```julia
julia> first(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr))
5
```

but that's somewhat bulky. So, I propose that we add `first(f, itr)`, which should work for arbitrary iterators. While we're at it, I suppose we should also add `first(f, itr, n::Integer)`. That would be expected to return an array, since `first(itr, n)` returns an array:

```julia
julia> first(itr, 2)
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 6
```

So, I suppose if one wanted `first(f, itr, n)` to return an iterator, they would have to use the following instead:

```julia
julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2)
Base.Iterators.Take{Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}}(Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}(isodd, Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)), 2)

julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2) |> collect
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 7
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1279763509","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996#issuecomment-1279763509","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996","id":1279763509,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5MR6Q1","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-15T15:07:32Z","updated_at":"2022-10-15T15:07:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Change the name of `findfirst` to `findfirstindex` or `findfirstkey`, and then have `findfirst` return a value rather than a key or index. And similarly for `findlast` and `findall`. Although with `findall` you would have the thorny question of what kind of object to return---an array or some kind of iterator.

In an ideal world, I would have something like the following:

- `findall` returns an iterator of values. If there are no matches, then the iterator is empty.
- `findfirst` returns a `Union{Nothing, Some{T}}`, where `T` is the element type of the input iterator.
- `findfirstindex` behaves the same as `findfirst` in Julia 1.x.

...and similarly for `findlast`/`findlastindex`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1279763509/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

- Rename `findlast` to `findlastindex`.

Related discussion in #44996.

I find the new auto suggestion mode *very* distracting. making ipython basically unusable.

How can I disable this?
(I would appreciate if you did not enable new features to the UI by default in the future)

From the documation or a grep of *.py files of IPython I could not find how to do it.

I tried in my ipython config file

c = get_config()
...
c.enable_auto_suggest = False
```
but with no success.

Throwing an error would probably be preferable.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262258552","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352#issuecomment-1262258552","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352","id":1262258552,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LPIl4","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-29T13:11:12Z","updated_at":"2022-09-29T13:11:12Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Look at the difference closely, it's 2 and not 1.

A little respect, please. I'm not blind. If the intended semantics of `map!` are that `f` is applied sequentially, then the output is correct.

> so the behaviour as described in the documentation does not hold.

This is the problem. The docstring for `map!` is so vague that it's impossible to know which of the two possible behaviors is intended. Basically we need an authority figure to chime in and tell us which of the following two semantic definitions for `map!` is the intended one:

1) Transform `collection` by applying `f` to each element, and *then* store the result in `collection`.
2) Sequentially apply `f` to `collection`, with results sequentially stored in `destination`.

Given the possibility of memory aliasing and given that `map!` is supposed to operate in-place, I think definition 2) is the only reasonable one. 

Note that the docstring for `map` can get away with being a little vague, since there's no mutation involved, but for `map!` we need to be more precise.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262258552/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

julia> x = [1,2]

 1
 2

julia> map!(i -> i + 1, x, view(x, 2:-1:1))

```

This is silently incorrect. The result should be `[3, 2]`.
Throwing an error would probably be preferable.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262531940","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352#issuecomment-1262531940","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46352","id":1262531940,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LQLVk","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-29T16:39:05Z","updated_at":"2022-09-29T16:39:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> It would be highly strange if load/store order was part of the documented API. That seem like such a low-level detail and seems odd to expose at the API level.

There's nothing odd about exposing sequential behavior at the API level. Look at `foldl`, `foldr`, and just about anything related to iteration. I think "sequentially" and "iteratively" are close to synonymous, and there's plenty of iteration in the language. How about I change the wording of definition 2) to the following?

2) Iteratively apply `f` to `collection`. At each iteration, store the return value of `f` at the corresponding location in `destination`.

As I mentioned already, due to the mutation involved, the semantics of `map!` needs to be made more precise. And due to the in-place nature of `map!`, the semantics of definition 1) are not really tenable.

> Further, if it is exposed, then the current implementation is still buggy, since we must guarantee that it never SIMDs.

As far as I'm aware, there is no interface for declaring that SIMD is not allowed, so we have to rely on unit tests. Here's the code for the [primary `map!` method](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/9fd408723aab0974f399b8ffc952af4b4d20b6f7/base/abstractarray.jl#L3210):

```julia
function map!(f::F, dest::AbstractArray, A::AbstractArray) where F
    for (i,j) in zip(eachindex(dest),eachindex(A))
        val = f(@inbounds A[j])
        @inbounds dest[i] = val
    end
    return dest
end
```

I'm not a compiler guy either, but the docstring for `@simd` says "The object iterated over in a `@simd` for loop should be a one-dimensional range." Of course, the loop in `map!` is not annotated with `@simd`, but this leads me to believe that the Julia compiler would not attempt SIMD on this loop, since the object being iterated over is an `Iterators.Zip` object, not a range object. Even if the object being iterated over was a range, I wonder if this is one of the situations where the compiler recognizes that it is not safe to change the order of iteration, and therefore does not attempt SIMD. Anyhow, with a decent test suite for this behavior (which we don't have) and CI/CD on multiple platforms (which we do have), we can be reasonably confident that SIMD is not happening.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262531940/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979","id":1391155721,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5S61oJ","number":46979,"title":"ismutable says strings are mutable in Julia","user":{"login":"udohjeremiah","id":98605632,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeCaQA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98605632?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah","html_url":"https://github.com/udohjeremiah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-09-29T16:28:07Z","updated_at":"2022-09-30T01:24:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The doc section about `ismutable` says:

help?> ismutable
search: ismutable ismutabletype isimmutable

  ismutable(v) -> Bool

  Return true if and only if value v is mutable. See Mutable Composite Types for a
  discussion of immutability. Note that this function works on values, so if you
  give it a type, it will tell you that a value of DataType is mutable.

Standing on this word: **Return true if and only if value v is mutable** and also what the doc says [here](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/strings/#man-strings) about strings:
> As in Java, strings are immutable: the value of an AbstractString object cannot be changed. To construct a different string value, you construct a new string from parts of other strings.

is there a reason for this still returning `true`:
```julia

true
```

The above leads to bugs in code where strings are technically known not to be "changeable", but then `ismutable` still returns `true`. Moreover the `ismutabletype` returns `true` for strings as well:
```julia

true
```

I'm beginning to think this behaviour for strings is similar to `BigInt` and `BigFloat` where same process happens of `ismutable` and `ismutabletype` returning `true` happens also. IMHO, this shouldn't be the case for strings.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262997410","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979#issuecomment-1262997410","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46979","id":1262997410,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5LR8-i","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-30T01:24:05Z","updated_at":"2022-09-30T01:24:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"While I agree that it would be nice if `ismutable` returned `false` for strings, my understanding is that `ismutable` is not very reliable in production code. For custom types, it seems that `ismutable` boils down to whether your type was defined as a `struct` or a `mutable struct`. Consider this example:

```julia
struct A{T} <: AbstractVector{T}
    x::Vector{T}
end

Base.size(a::A) = size(a.x)
Base.getindex(a::A, i) = a.x[i]
Base.setindex!(a::A, v, i) = (a.x[i] = v)
```

```julia
julia> a = A([1, 2, 3])
3-element A{Int64}:
 1
 2
 3

julia> a[2] = 100
100

julia> a
3-element A{Int64}:
   1
 100
   3

julia> ismutable(a)
false
```

So we see that vectors of the custom type `A` are mutable, but `ismutable` returns `false`, because the underlying `struct` is immutable. 🤷‍♂️ ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1262997410/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Quote from Keno [on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/whats-so-mutable-about-symbol-s/8094/10?u=cameronbieganek):

> Well, the reflection functions answer questions about the system, not convention.

In other words, the reflection functions like `ismutable` and `isbits` are not really designed for everyday programming, they're designed to answer questions about the underlying objects.

> So what do we use in production code to determine what's mutable and what isn't?

If you're only concerned with arrays, you can use `ArrayInterface.ismutable`, from ArrayInterface.jl, which is different from `Base.ismutable`. However, not all third party packages that implement array types necessarily use ArrayInterface.jl.

Otherwise, it depends on the context. You can add specific dispatches for the (abstract) types that you know should work. Or you can take the "let it fail" approach. In other words, just use mutation in your function, and if the input object is mutable it will work, if it's not mutable it will throw an error. That's generally the approach that generic code has to take, since there is no `IsMutable` trait.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1263567444/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
julia> for f in (:foo, :bar)
           @eval begin
               "This is the $(nameof($f)) function."
               $f(x) = 2x
           end
       end

help?> foo
search: foo floor pointer_from_objref OverflowError RoundFromZero unsafe_copyto! functionloc StackOverflowError @functionloc OutOfMemoryError

  This is the foo function.

help?> bar
search: bar baremodule SubArray GlobalRef clipboard BitArray backtrace BitMatrix catch_backtrace AbstractRange AbstractArray AbstractIrrational

  This is the bar function.
```

So we could use that to add `foo(::Missing)` docstrings for all the math functions [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/6026b5ff2a8035fc9d70af30123af9a6e722c585/base/math.jl#L1364) and [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/6026b5ff2a8035fc9d70af30123af9a6e722c585/base/math.jl#L1367).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1114317540/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

values","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-01T20:46:40Z","updated_at":"2022-05-01T20:46:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I think that every function that handles `missing` values should have its handling of `missing` values documented in its docstring. There are two possible ways to do this:

- Add discussion of the handling of `missing` values to the generic `foo(x)` docstring.
- Add separate docstrings for all the `foo(::Missing)`, `bar(::Missing, ::Any)`, etc, methods.
    - This approach won't work for methods that take iterators, like `all`, `any`, `maximum`, and `minimum`.

Functions that handle missing values currently do not have fully specified APIs, since their docstrings make no mention of `missing` values. My position is that docstrings should always strive to fully specify the API of their functions, so that in theory a person could write an entire application by referring only to the docstrings. In other words, you shouldn't have to run `foo(missing)` in the REPL in order to determine whether `foo` handles `missing` values.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45142/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45142/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

code"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":35,"created_at":"2021-06-12T13:47:18Z","updated_at":"2022-04-02T00:09:56Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Original Title: **Change `fill([0, 0], 2)` behavior for 2.0**

The behavior of `fill` when its first argument is an array is to create an array of arrays, where each of the elements is a reference to the exact same array in memory. This is counterintuitive to many users (see Discourse links below), and it seems kind of pointless. I propose that in 2.0 `fill` with an array input should fill the resulting array with *copies* of the input array.

Below are some examples where this came up on Discourse. There may be more.

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/simple-question-about-assignment-to-a-vector-of-vectors/62744
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fill-anarray-2-behaviour/22429
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/initialization-of-array-of-arrays-with-fill-ones-1-2-2-only-one-vector-is-created/48048
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-can-i-fill-an-array-with-empty-2d-arrays/60895","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209/reactions","total_count":14,"+1":3,"-1":11,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1086423923","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209#issuecomment-1086423923","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209","id":1086423923,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5AwYNz","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-02T00:09:56Z","updated_at":"2022-04-02T00:09:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Given {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996","id":1205792072,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5H3u1I","number":44996,"title":"Add `first(f, itr)`","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-04-15T18:46:42Z","updated_at":"2022-04-15T18:46:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I often find myself wishing that we had a version of `findfirst(f, A)` that just returned the first `x` for which `f(x)` is true, rather than returning the index. Granted, you could just write that as `A[findfirst(f, A)]`, but that's less elegant for long array names, like

```julia
very_long_name[findfirst(f, very_long_name)]
```

Furthermore, `findfirst(f, A)` does not work for arbitrary iterators:

```julia
julia> itr = Iterators.drop(1:8, 4)
Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)

julia> findfirst(isodd, itr)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching keys(::Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}})
```

We do have the following option,

```julia
julia> first(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr))
5
```

but that's somewhat bulky. So, I propose that we add `first(f, itr)`, which should work for arbitrary iterators. While we're at it, I suppose we should also add `first(f, itr, n::Integer)`. That would be expected to return an array, since `first(itr, n)` returns an array:

```julia
julia> first(itr, 2)
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 6
```

So, I suppose if one wanted `first(f, itr, n)` to return an iterator, they would have to use the following instead:

```julia
julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2)
Base.Iterators.Take{Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}}(Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}(isodd, Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)), 2)

julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2) |> collect
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 7
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

often find myself wishing that we had a version of `findfirst(f, A)` that just returned the first `x` for which `f(x)` is true, rather than returning the index. Granted, you could just write that as `A[findfirst(f, A)]`, but that's less elegant for long array names, like

```julia
very_long_name[findfirst(f, very_long_name)]
```

Furthermore, `findfirst(f, A)` does not work for arbitrary iterators:

```julia
julia> itr = Iterators.drop(1:8, 4)
Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)

julia> findfirst(isodd, itr)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching keys(::Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}})
```

We do have the following option,

```julia
julia> first(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr))
5
```

but that's somewhat bulky. So, I propose that we add `first(f, itr)`, which should work for arbitrary iterators. While we're at it, I suppose we should also add `first(f, itr, n::Integer)`. That would be expected to return an array, since `first(itr, n)` returns an array:

```julia
julia> first(itr, 2)
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 6
```

So, I suppose if one wanted `first(f, itr, n)` to return an iterator, they would have to use the following instead:

```julia
julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2)
Base.Iterators.Take{Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}}(Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}(isodd, Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)), 2)

julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2) |> collect
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 5
 7
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44996/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
julia> first(itr, 2)

 6
```

So, I suppose if one wanted `first(f, itr, n)` to return an iterator, they would have to use the following instead:

julia> Iterators.take(Iterators.filter(isodd, itr), 2)
Base.Iterators.Take{Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}}(Base.Iterators.Filter{typeof(isodd), Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}}(isodd, Base.Iterators.Drop{UnitRange{Int64}}(1:8, 4)), 2)

 5
 7

- `findfirst(f, itr; value=true)`

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106","id":1217457667,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5IkO4D","number":45106,"title":"Improve error message in range function","user":{"login":"chadagreene","id":15901019,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1OTAxMDE5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/15901019?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene","html_url":"https://github.com/chadagreene","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chadagreene/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-04-27T14:39:14Z","updated_at":"2022-04-27T16:21:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"A Type error in the `length` argument of `range` currently produces this error message: 
```julia
julia> range(0,1,length=1e3)
ERROR: TypeError: in keyword argument length, expected Union{Nothing, Integer}, got a value of type Float64
```
The current error message: 
1. fails to mention that the error is related to the `range` function, 
2. is somewhat cryptic about what exactly the source of the problem is,  
3. offers no guidance for what users can do about the problem, and 
4. is a series of sentence fragments that don't actually form a meaningful sentence. 

In my opinion, **all** error messages should _always_ be written in the following order: 
1. Identify the location of the problem. 
2. Describe the technical cause of the problem (in human language). 
3. Conclude with some gentle advice for what can be done about the problem. 

Using the three-step formula above, I propose the following rewrite, or something similar: 

```julia 
Line 5: ERROR in range(): Keyword argument length was entered as type Float64, but should be of type Integer or Nothing.
```

A discussion of the `range` error message is on the Discourse site [here](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/why-does-scientific-notation-break-the-range-function/80014). ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1111201133","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106#issuecomment-1111201133","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45106","id":1111201133,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5CO5Vt","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-27T16:21:29Z","updated_at":"2022-04-27T16:21:29Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think the title of this issue could be made more general. Something like this:

> Improve error messages when a keyword argument type assertion fails.

Here's the generic MWE:

```julia
julia> foo(; x::Union{Nothing, Integer}=nothing) = x
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> foo(; x=0.5)
ERROR: TypeError: in keyword argument x, expected Union{Nothing, Integer}, got a value of type Float64
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[11]:1
```

It seems to me that there are two main ways to improve this message:

1. Include the function name in the error message. (The function name `foo` does not occur in the stacktrace because the error is technically not happening inside of `foo`.)
2. If the type that is being asserted is not concrete, then change the message to, e.g. `... expected a subtype of Union{Nothing, Integer} ...`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1111201133/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Consider this type:

```julia
Union{Nothing, Integer, String, AbstractVector{<:AbstractFloat}}
```

That would have to be translated to something like this:

```
... expected a value of type Nothing or String, or a value of a type that is a
subtype of Integer or AbstractVector{<:AbstractFloat} ...
```

I guess that's not horrible. :)

"value of a type that is a subtype of" seems a little awkward, but I can't think of a better way to say it that is actually precise.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1111234336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

messages"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2022-04-27T14:39:14Z","updated_at":"2022-04-27T17:10:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"A Type error in the `length` argument of `range` currently produces this error message: 
```julia
julia> range(0,1,length=1e3)
ERROR: TypeError: in keyword argument length, expected Union{Nothing, Integer}, got a value of type Float64
```
The current error message: 
1. fails to mention that the error is related to the `range` function, 
2. is somewhat cryptic about what exactly the source of the problem is,  
3. offers no guidance for what users can do about the problem, and 
4. is a series of sentence fragments that don't actually form a meaningful sentence. 

In my opinion, **all** error messages should _always_ be written in the following order: 
1. Identify the location of the problem. 
2. Describe the technical cause of the problem (in human language). 
3. Conclude with some gentle advice for what can be done about the problem. 

Using the three-step formula above, I propose the following rewrite, or something similar: 

```julia 
Line 5: ERROR in range(): Keyword argument length was entered as type Float64, but should be of type Integer or Nothing.
```

A discussion of the `range` error message is on the Discourse site [here](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/why-does-scientific-notation-break-the-range-function/80014). ```
ERROR: TypeError: in keyword argument `length`, expected Union{Nothing, Integer} ...
```

In other words, does the phrase `keyword argument length` mean "the length of a keyword argument", or does it mean "the keyword argument named `length`" ?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1111255577/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
... expected Union{Nothing, Integer}, got a value of type Float64
```

Note that it is asymmetrical. After the comma it says "value of type Y", but before the comma it just says "X", where X and Y are two different types. It should actually say the following to be precise:

```
expected a value of a type that is a subtype of Union{Nothing, Integer}, got a value of type Float64
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137","id":413092463,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU0MTMwOTI0NjM=","number":31137,"title":"Optimized methods for `mapreduce(f, vcat, xs)` and `mapreduce(f, hcat, xs)`","user":{"login":"oxinabox","id":5127634,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUxMjc2MzQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5127634?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox","html_url":"https://github.com/oxinabox","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2019-02-21T19:43:50Z","updated_at":"2022-02-09T20:14:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"given we had https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/21672
which makes `reduce(vcat` and `reduce(hcat` fast.

I had assumed it also applied to `mapreduce` of these operations.
But it clearly does not.

```
julia> @btime reduce(vcat, $datas)
  87.049 μs (2 allocations: 117.27 KiB)

julia> @btime mapreduce(identity, vcat, $datas);
  5.372 ms (10005 allocations: 37.04 MiB)

julia> @btime reduce(vcat, map(identity, $datas));
  93.212 μs (5 allocations: 156.42 KiB)
```

Right now, if the 2nd arg is `vcat` or `hcat` it is faster to call `reduce(op, map(f, xs))` than `mapreduce(f, op, xs)`.
And maybe that would be a solid first move to fix this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1034152956","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137#issuecomment-1034152956","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/31137","id":1034152956,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM49o-v8","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-02-09T20:14:24Z","updated_at":"2022-02-09T20:14:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I just bumped into this.
